questions.question: A shop deliberately racks long, freshly demagnetized steel bars east–west rather than north–south. The reason is:
options: East–west storage keeps the bars cooler and slows corrosion
The earth's field is strongest in the east–west direction
Bars stored aligned with the earth's roughly 0.5-gauss field can slowly acquire a weak longitudinal magnetization
North–south storage interferes with overhead crane magnets
questions.explanation: The earth's field, weak as it is at about half a gauss, never switches off — steel stored, machined, or repeatedly handled while aligned north–south can gradually pick up a weak longitudinal field, so some shops rack long stock east–west.

questions.question: The direction of current through a straight conductor is reversed. The magnetic field surrounding the conductor:
options: Disappears entirely
Reverses its circular direction around the conductor
Doubles in strength
Changes from circular to longitudinal
questions.explanation: The field always circles the conductor at right angles to the current — right-hand rule. Reversing the current reverses the circulation; only the geometry of the conductor (straight versus coiled) changes the field's shape.

questions.question: Winding a magnetizing cable into a multi-turn coil around a shaft produces:
options: A longitudinal field along the coil axis, suited to finding transverse discontinuities in the shaft
A circular field inside the shaft, suited to finding longitudinal discontinuities
No field unless the shaft touches the cable
A field that exists only outside the coil
questions.explanation: The circular fields of the individual turns add together into one longitudinal field running down the coil axis. Flux along the part's length crosses transverse flaws at a high angle, which is what makes them show.

questions.question: Placing a steel keeper bar across the poles of a horseshoe magnet noticeably weakens its external pull because:
options: The keeper permanently demagnetizes the horseshoe
Steel blocks magnetic fields the way lead blocks radiation
The keeper reverses the magnet's polarity
The keeper completes a low-reluctance flux path, keeping the flux inside metal instead of in the air gap
questions.explanation: Flux always prefers the lowest-reluctance path. With a keeper bridging the poles, the magnetic circuit closes through steel — like a closed ring — so little flux remains in the air to attract external objects.

questions.question: A forging is still glowing from heat treatment, well above 770 °C. MT cannot be performed because:
options: The particles would explode on contact
Above the Curie point the steel is paramagnetic — its domain alignment is destroyed and it cannot be magnetized until it cools
Hot steel carries too much flux density
Electrical current cannot flow through hot steel
questions.explanation: Above the Curie point (about 770 °C for iron) thermal agitation destroys domain alignment and the material behaves paramagnetically. As a practical matter you would never get close to that limit anyway — even high-temperature dry powders are typically rated only to around 600 °F.

questions.question: Under the same 50-oersted magnetizing force, material X develops 10,000 gauss and material Y develops 60 gauss. The correct conclusion is:
options: Both materials have the same permeability because H is the same
Material Y is ferromagnetic and X is paramagnetic
Material X has a permeability of about 200 versus about 1.2 for Y — X is ferromagnetic and testable with MT
Material X is at the Curie point
questions.explanation: Permeability is µ = B/H: 10,000 ÷ 50 = 200 for X, while 60 ÷ 50 = 1.2 for Y. The enormous difference in flux carried under identical magnetizing force is precisely what separates ferromagnetic materials from everything else.

questions.question: Which set lists the three ferromagnetic elements?
options: Iron, nickel, cobalt
Iron, copper, aluminum
Nickel, titanium, bismuth
Cobalt, gold, tungsten
questions.explanation: Iron, nickel, and cobalt are the ferromagnetic elements; their atoms lock into aligned domains. Copper and bismuth are diamagnetic, and aluminum and titanium are paramagnetic.

questions.question: Compared with annealed low-carbon steel, a hardened high-carbon steel typically shows a hysteresis loop that is:
options: Narrower — it demagnetizes almost by itself
A straight line through the origin
Identical — loop shape does not depend on the alloy or its condition
Wider and fatter — higher retentivity and coercive force, making the residual method possible and demagnetization deliberate work
questions.explanation: Hard, high-carbon steels are magnetically hard: fat loop, high retentivity, high coercive force. They hold a residual field strong enough for residual-method inspection and resist demagnetization. Soft annealed steels show thin loops and need the continuous method.

questions.question: Which statement correctly pairs the field-measurement tool with its job?
options: Pie gauge — quantitative gauss readings at the surface
Hall-effect gaussmeter — quantitative tangential field; pie gauge — qualitative field direction and adequacy; pocket field indicator — residual checks after demagnetization
Pocket field indicator — sets the machine amperage automatically
Hall-effect gaussmeter — measures particle bath concentration
questions.explanation: The gaussmeter reads field strength quantitatively (procedures commonly target a tangential field on the order of 30–60 gauss), the pie gauge and shims answer the qualitative question of direction and adequacy, and the simple field indicator verifies residual fields, commonly to 3 gauss or less.

questions.question: During an examination, one indication appears broad, weak, and fuzzy-edged rather than sharp and dense. The most likely cause is:
options: A tight crack open to the surface
Too little amperage for any indication to form
A discontinuity just below the surface — its leakage field spreads and weakens before reaching the particles
Particles applied too generously
questions.explanation: Depth controls appearance: surface cracks interrupt flux right at the particle layer and draw sharp, tightly held lines, while near-surface discontinuities leak a broader, weaker field by the time it reaches the surface — the classic fuzzy indication.
